summary: O'Neil argues that almost all of big data is devoted to surveillance, tracing the progression from corporate data collection to defense contractor tracking software to educational data mining of children. She examines how corporations like Yahoo frame surveillance as 'personalized experience,' how Raytheon's Riot software can track and predict individual movements using social media data, and how recent FERPA amendments have opened children's educational data to third-party contractors. Writing en route to a Congressional hearing on big data, she calls for stronger privacy protections, noting that anonymization doesn't work, the line between sensitive and nonsensitive data has blurred, and the most vulnerable people are least aware of the threat.
key_points: Corporate data collection is fundamentally surveillance rebranded as 'personalized experience,' with companies like Yahoo explicitly refusing to discard any data
Defense contractor Raytheon's Riot software represents the militarization of social media data, capable of tracking and predicting individual movements using Facebook, Twitter, and Foursquare
FERPA amendments in 2008 and 2011 expanded third-party access to student data, with contractors like inBloom unable to guarantee data security, making children the most surveilled generation
Anonymization doesn't work with large databases, and nonsensitive data can be used to infer sensitive information, collapsing the distinction between the two
Privacy policy must focus not just on data collection but on data usage—companies providing loans or insurance should not have unrestricted access to purchased personal information
money_quotes: Almost all of big data is devoted to surveillance.
Thanks for telling us consumers how great this is, Todd.
Our approach is not to throw any data away.
No doubt about it: our children are well on their way to being the most stalked generation.
The very people who should be worried the most about their data being exposed and freely available are the ones who don't understand the threat.
Merely saying that people should protect their data more is utterly insufficient.
Just because you have a good idea of my age, race, education level, income, and HIV status doesn't mean you should be able to use that information against me whenever you want.
key_insight: Big data is fundamentally a surveillance infrastructure, and protecting privacy requires regulating not just data collection but data usage, especially for vulnerable populations like children who cannot advocate for themselves.
